speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Venezia, you were telling us that you went to work for Carl's Shoes in Los Angeles. And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working over there, making overtime.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were-- and your wife, Nina?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a tailor. What do they call them? Tailor, too? She was very good on it. We had big projects to do it. But we couldn't finish because Daisy got passed away. Then she got sick for two years like this. She got operated and so on. Because the doctors saw her so strong and so beautiful, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they said, we're going to take a chance to operate on her. They operate on her head. But they give us-- they give her about three or four months to live. She lived another year. She was a very strong woman. Never sick. And it happened. That-- she was dreaming about her girl all the time, you know. And in the middle of the night, she was waking up, felling down, start shaking like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- I suffer enough over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have other children before your wife died? Did you have other children besides your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: The two boys. I had them with same wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you tell us the names of your two sons?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you before.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the tape. Tell us the names of your two sons.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. One-- the one is-- who got killed was Isaac Venezia. Venezia. And the other one is the one in the picture over here, Albert Venezia.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your wife-- your first wife die? What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wish I could ask Athena. I don't remember. I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe-- I forget how many years. I'm 45 years over here. I was five, six years with her. Some time ago, about 37 years ago, 38 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: About 1959?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that. '58, '59.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your oldest son, Isaac-- or Ike, as you call-- as you called him-- when did he pass away? When did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: When did she pass?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he die, your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 15 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: About 1981?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that. I-- I got somewhere-- I marked down somewhere a-- where the list they send to me from the mortuary send me every year. It was on December, something like that. Maybe '80, '81.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after you lost your little girl and your wife and your son, and-- did you remarry?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remarry after mine wife dies. So I had the two little boys. They were four or five-- five years old. So-- and I married to Athena, and we were living together, her two children and mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married the second time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We should ask Athena.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it about 1965, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Might be. Maybe. I don't remember dates, and I don't care about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what else-- what other kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over here? After I was in Carl's Shoes, I went out with a neighbor of mine, a Greek guy born over here. We was painter contractor. So from $1.37 I was getting-- or $1.65 I was getting from Carl's Shoes, I was getting $2 and something in painting, which I didn't have no idea in. And I worked with this guy about six months. And then I went out by myself, contacting myself. Insurer But it was very difficult because I did not know the language. The only thing I was working in painting was with other Greek people over here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after that, I don't know how many years I worked there. And then my wife passed away, and so when I got married to Athena, she said, why don't we get a grocery store somewhere? So I borrowed money from here and there and from the Jewish family. Money. I got my first grocery store, and I start to make my life. After all of the things that you went through in your years after the war, did you suffer nightmares and dreams about what you went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time. All the time with the Germans. And then when I open mine eyes, I can't believe that it was-- was a dream. It looked like it was-- was real, and was-- I was running, running, and sweating. All the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever talked to other people about this besides the people within your community?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Only with friends and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you still working today?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm retired. It's been already-- I'm retired for 10 years already.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you do with your time? What do you like to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing I like to do is always work with mine hands. It's no problem for me for anything in the house happen. No matter what. Like you saw it, what I build over here. Anything you see over-- over here. Tiles and even those tiles over here in the hall. And the-- the new bathroom I got. The kitchen. The kitchen. Yeah. And over here, the painting, roofing. I do anything. Even when I go in Greece for vacation-- because it warm up, we came back from vacation. We got a little home over there right on the beach. We're going-- for 18 years now, we're going to Greece for four months. Even over there, I can find something to do. I straighten up all the things when I saw it which is not in the right place, no matter what. Big or small. I fix it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still in contact with your brother Shlomo today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. A-- a month and a half ago, I were-- he came to Greece to-- for 10 days with his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is he living now?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Rome.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you, Mr. Venezia, since you are one of the very, very few true eyewitnesses to Hitler's final solution, is there any kind of a message or anything you'd like to say to future generations or to people who might see this tape at some later time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Carol, what can I say? Never again. It's impossible to hope in such a thing. Impossible. And now with the state of Israel, we got a country. Not only country, we got a strong country. And every day it's going to get stronger and stronger and stronger so nobody will touch us. No man in this world, if you're Christian, if you're Jewish, if you're Muslim, or you're-- whatever you are, they all human being in this world. All human beings. It will never happen again what happened with those animals. That's what I can say.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to thank you for taking the time to give us your testimony today. We really appreciate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thank you too, and all those people working with you. Because I've got a big thing out of my chest. I was looking good for so many years to testify because I-- I know I won't live so long to be able to testify again, and especial for the people-- we are six or seven, maybe, living now. Or 10, the most. I don't think so. That is going-- we're going to be able to testify again for such a thing because we are [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] witnesses still alive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And all that thing what I told you, maybe I don't remember everything. I testify the most of the things. But still [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's never too much to testify. Because if you remember, every day you were passing by is impossible. Like one day, for example, I was in a crematoria working in the crematoria tomb. [CRYING] So we saw those Russian pull people, victims of-- of the war-- they were in the military service. They brought it to us. They didn't have no-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to-- to bring those militares to us. They were-- they were fighting for their country. Like the Germans, they were fighting for their country too. So they were militares. They brought it to us. So one day, I saw two, three of those Russians. They brought in a kind of a German shepherd dog, dead. Who knows, that dog, how did he go to the-- to the electric wires over there, and he died. Right away, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they are-- skin the dog, and so on. And they boil it, and we ate every bite over-- every piece of dog meat. This is what I remember with those Russians. Of course, they got killed all over by the end. But this was Malenkov, was the worst thing I ever-- I ever seen. With a cane, hit him with his hand-- his head. The cane broke up, and they still hit him with a broken cane until he killed him with a cane, hit him on his head.

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- those are something unbelievable. I'm-- I'm testifying of this. But I saw it with mine eyes. Can somebody imagine those moments we lived in it? No. It's impossible. Thank you. Thank you very much. We're going to look at some of your photographs now. Tell us who this is.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is mine father, Isaac Venezia. I don't remember exactly when that picture was taken. Of course, I was a-- a little boy. But I believe it was in 1918 or '20, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Born in Thessaloniki. And he was a barber.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, I got it from a neighbor-- an old neighbor where I was living in Thessaloniki after the liberation. I think the neighbor got some pictures from my home, and she gave it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Dudune Venezia, born also in Thessaloniki. And I don't know exactly when the picture was taken. But I believe was in 19-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1920? 1918. Something like that. This is a picture taken maybe a year or six months after the liberation. On the left, I, myself. Morris Venezia. On the right is my cousin, Dario Gabbai.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Both born in Thessaloniki. This picture is taken in Athens, in 1946. This is a picture of mineself, Morris Venezia, taken in Athens in 1947. On my left, the picture of Dario Gabbai, my cousin. And on the right is myself, Morris Venezia. Walking around the streets of Athens in 1947. This is myself on the left and my wife Athena after we got mine married. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it about 1965?

speaker: interviewee
text: '65, '66. Something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you married? Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: We marry here in Los Angeles. And the picture is taken here in Los Angeles. This picture, it shows the crematoria number three, which was sent to me from Poland from mine brother Shlomo, who was there to testify with some children of the Italian schools. Was taken 1992. This picture, sent to me by mine brother, Shlomo, in 1994. When he went with an Italian schools and professors to Auschwitz to testify and narrate to the children of the Italians schools-- Jewish-- what was happening, and showing over there the number what's got his arm with a number in this kind of a monument they got him over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now who is this in the picture again?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture is mine brother, Shlomo. This picture is taken in 1989. To the left is Rachel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the little one is Elizabeth. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Sharon, wife of my son Albert Venezia. And the young-- young man over there is Sharon's son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the right, the handsome gentleman with the tie?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right-- on the right mine-- mine son, Albert.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Los Angeles. OK. This is a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the way I did it fast, what I can remember is a number-- crematoria number one was three or four ovens. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't-- I didn't work over there. Just in that crematoria we would burn the remaining 100 people who escape from the same-- same revolution we did. After that is this-- was a street. After the street over there was going the guards every night. They were going to get their post with the guns and machine guns. After this street was crematoria number two, which over there we got [INAUDIBLE] that right away with the going on revolution, we can say. The only crematoria who lived in that kind of revolution. After that was the Canada-- Canada commando, which they were taking care most of the women for the clothing. I don't know what they were going-- they were-- what they were doing or where they were going. After that was a street. And after this street was crematoria three, which the crematoria three is got bombed up. And everybody else working in this crematoria got killed by-- by gun. And right a-- right away bend the-- built-- bent up on the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this kind of horse they had over there in the trenches. On the trench he had over there. After crematoria three is number four, which in number four they got killed too in the-- this kind of a revolution we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you worked first in crematoria number three?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then from number three--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to two.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in two when the revolt took place? Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And then at the crematoria one to go and burn the people who run away from the electric wires.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. Thank you very much.
